revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Huri (Ainu legendary bird)]][[ja:フリカムイ]]
'''Huri Kamuy''' (フリカムイ) or simply '''Huri''' (フリ) is a giant bird featured in {{ill|Ainu folklore|ja|アイヌ民話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu folklore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Characteristics ==
Huri Kamuy is described as a colossal bird with a single wing spanning about seven ri (approximately 30 kilometers). It was said to dwell in caves upstream of the {{ill|Tokachi River|en|Tokachi River|qid=Q3491135|lt=Tokachi River|1=Tokachi River}} or along the coasts of {{ill|Abashiri Subprefecture|ja|網走支庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abashiri Subprefecture|1=Abashiri Subprefecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13377526}}. For food, it ventured to the sea, where it caught {{ill|Chum salmon|en|Chum salmon|qid=Q475211|lt=salmon|1=Chum salmon}} and {{ill|Whale|en|Whale|qid=Q1865281|lt=whales|1=Whale}} to eat.
In the dialect of {{ill|Asahikawa|en|Asahikawa|qid=Q200740|lt=Asahikawa|1=Asahikawa}}’s {{ill|Chikabumi area|ja|近文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikabumi area|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the bird is referred to as '''Hūrin''' (hurin) or '''Hūrin Tono''' (hurin tono). In {{ill|Bihoro, Hokkaido|en|Bihoro, Hokkaido|qid=Q1355074|lt=Bihoro|1=Bihoro, Hokkaido}}, it is recorded as '''Hiwri''' (hiwri) or '''Siwri''' (siwri). Other variations of the name include '''Furīkamuy''', '''Furī''', and '''Furyū'''.
== Legends ==
Originally, Huri Kamuy did not harm humans, and people avoided approaching or interfering with its habitat. However, one day, a woman ventured into the mountains to forage for food and crossed a stream with mu11y feet, inadvertently contaminating Huri Kamuy's drinking water.
Angered by this disturbance, Huri Kamuy's temperament soured, and it began attacking humans and animals, destroying trees and plants, and even demolishing houses. Its sheer power was evident as its wing flaps generated gusts strong enough to uproot trees and blow away buildings. Humans retaliated against its rampages, but this only deepened its anger and made its destruction even worse. Huri Kamuy came to be feared as a demon-like creature.
Eventually, a man from a region near {{ill|Lake_Shikotsu|en|Lake_Shikotsu|qid=Q1196295|lt=Lake Shikotsu|1=Lake Shikotsu}}, called Ruikayar, ended the chaos by slaying Huri Kamuy with a spear, bringing an end to its reign of terror.
== References == * Miyoko Matsutani, *Nihon no Densetsu (Shimo)*, Kodansha Bunko, 1975, pp. 259–265. * {{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|1=Kenji Murakami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11522709}}, *Yōkai Jiten*, Mainichi Shimbun, 2000, p. 299. * {{ill|Mashiho Chiri|en|Mashiho Chiri|qid=Q11584669|lt=Chiri Mashio|1=Mashiho Chiri}}, *Bunrui Ainu-go Jiten (Dōbutsu-hen)*, Heibonsha, 1975, p. 216.
== See also == * {{ill|List of Japanese yōkai|ja|日本の妖怪の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Japanese yōkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の妖怪一覧|1=List of legendary creatures from Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2731245}} * {{ill|Rātoshikamuy|ja|ラートシカムイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rātoshikamuy|qq=}} * {{ill|Ipetam|ja|イペタム|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipetam|qq=}} – A legend suggests this blade was used to slay Huri Kamuy.
{{translated page|ja|フリカムイ|version=92107947|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:フリカムイ]]
